entry.title: Transfer Posting using Synchronous Goods Movement in EWM
entry.content:
Introduction:

In SAP EWM, almost all physical goods movements are processed through deliveries. While this approach provides end-to-end traceability and precise inventory tracking, it can at times become time-consuming and cumbersome and especially when issues arise that require immediate resolution.

In many cases, users also feel that not every goods movement requires delivery-based processing. To address these scenarios and simplify operational processes, SAP has Synchronous Goods Movement functionality in EWM. This feature enables posting of goods movements directly in MIGO without creating deliveries, reducing process complexity while still ensuring accurate inventory updates.

In today’s blog we will see how to  carry out transfer posting between storage locations with Synchronous Goods Movement in SAP EWM. But before we dive into the business process let us take a look at the configuration and setup to enable this functionality.

Configuration:

Step 1: Go to SPRO -> SCM EWM -> EWM -> Interfaces -> ERP Integration -> Goods Movement -> Activate Synchronous Goods Movement Posting.

Step 2: Go to SPRO -> SCM EWM -> EWM -> Interfaces -> ERP Integration -> Goods Movement -> Settings for Synchronous Goods Movements (MIGO) -> Activate Synchronous Goods Movements in the Warehouse.

Here we have three options.

Transfer Posting: Activating this for movements like 301, 311.

Goods Receipt: You can perform any goods receipt related movement type, such as 101, 561, 501, etc.

Goods Issue: For movements like 201, 261, etc

Note: For transferring from EWM to MM, or for posting a goods issue from EWM, make sure that the "No Goods Issue" field is unchecked for the storage type.

Testing:

Scenario 1: Transfer Posting from EWM to IM Storage Location (IMSL):

Go to transaction MIGO and select Transfer Posting -> Others, and here I am choosing the movement type as 311, which is Transfer Posting within storage location.

I am transferring the material from an EWM-managed storage location to an IM-managed storage location (IMSL).

Step 1: Enter the material, plant, storage location in Transfer posting tab and quantity in the Quantity tab

Step 2: In the warehouse management tab give the Storage bin where the stock lies.

Step 3: Click on post. Here instead of delivery generation, we get the material document posted and the warehouse task is generated in the background automatically.

Warehouse Document:

Stock Before Posting:

Stock After Posting:

Scenario 2: Transfer Posting from IMSL to EWM:

In this scenario, I am transferring the material from an IM-managed storage location to an EWM-managed storage location.

Step 1: Enter the Material, storage location in the Transfer Posting tab.

Step 2: Enter the quantity in the Quantity tab.

Step 3: In the Warehouse Management tab, enter the storage bin and select by quantity per HU, to select the packaging material and number of HU required.

Step 4: Now Post the document. Once it’s posted the material document is generated indicating the goods are received in the warehouse. The warehouse task is created in the background with the relevant HU.

Warehouse Document:

Stock Before Posting:

Stock After Posting:

Conclusion:

As shown above, Synchronous Goods Movement lets you post simple transfer postings, goods receipts, and goods issues directly from MIGO, eliminating the need to generate an EWM delivery for every movement. This is particularly useful for quick, ad-hoc stock corrections or transfers where full delivery-based traceability is not required, helping reduce process overhead while keeping EWM and ERP stock in sync. As always, evaluate this against your traceability and reporting requirements before enabling it broadly in a productive warehouse.

entry.title: Decoupling Unload and GR with POSC setup where TU is used for SAP EWM Inbound operations
entry.content:
This Blog post is intended to provider reader  how to decouple Unload and GR with POSC setup where TU is used for Inbound operations 

Business Scenario –

For one of our Automotive customers the Yard operator uses their third-party application which does all the transportation planning and EWM is expected to follow the Check in , Movement to Parking lot/Door, unloading, check out at the TU level as per events received from Third party. All the physical movements done with third party applications had to be in sync with TU status and business should be able to track exact TU status for Check in, Dock to door, Unloading etc. in SAP.

There was an ask to Decouple the Unloading and GR in SAP where-in we had Complex Unloading setup with steps for Unloading, Deconsolidate and Put away.

As per standard SAP setup, the Unloading Warehouse task Confirmation post Implicit Good receipt in the background however business ask was to decouple the same behavior.

We could achieve this setup by discarding Unloading WT creation step in the POSC and changing the Goods movement setup in SAP to Delivery level. Please go through the below details to understand current behavior and solution followed.

Current setup Behavior -

As per the screen capture, the TU is docked to the door and waiting for Unloading to be done. 

SAP copies Warehouse door from TU to IBD and same can be seen in the below screen shot. As per these values ,  System creates Unloading task from TU to ‘Staging Bay’

Next when We get event from third party Application that Unloading is finished, EWM Unloading task execution happens in the background and TU status gets updated with Unloading start/Finish. Once we set this Unloading finish at the TU system confirms unloading task in the background and simultaneously posts GR as well.

As per below screenshot , the unloading task is confirmed when we select ‘ Unload finished’ at the TU level and 2nd warehouse is created to move Nested HU with 2 products to Deconsolidation area.

Nested HU with 2 different products.

 

New Setup Behaviour-

Firstly, Configuration for Goods movement control must be set to ‘1. Goods movement to Storage Bin Specified in Delivery’.

New POSC is created with steps for Deconsolidation and Put away (Deleted Step IB01 for unloading)

Removed door from Staging area and door determination otherwise Goods movement bin is copied as door bin in the replicated IBD in EWM.

The Door bin is removed from  assignment of Door to SCU and storage bin.

Now as we can see in the Screen shot below, Staging bin is copied as ‘ Goods movement bin’

Later when TU is docked to door, the Door is copied from TU to IBD, but Goods movement bin is not updated and remains ‘ Staging bin’

As per TU status, Arrival at check point and docking to door is finished and now TU is ready to execute unloading.

As per our setup, we get unloading start and finish events from third part at this point of time and we set TU status accordingly.

To simulate above posting, execution is done in the RF as below.

Execution by RFUI menu--> Rec HU by ASN

 

 

As we can see the unloading , GR and WT creation status are all blanks.

Yard operator selects push button – ‘ UnLo’ and Unloading status is set to done.

The Unloading status is reflected in the in TU and most importantly the GR status remains open.

IBD shows ‘Unloading’ Completed but ‘Good receipt’ not started.

Next truck will move back to check point. TU departs from warehouse, and TU status is set to completed.

Now Logistic operators can do GR and rest of the warehouse operations at their own convenience . System’s only prerequisite to execute other warehouse operations with RF is to finish Unloading which is already done.

To execute GR, Logistic operator selects same RF menu -->Rec HU by ASN.

Unloading is already done so he will select GR and WT to move the pallet to Deconsolidation is created in the background.

 

Good receipt status is set to complete and Only Deconsolidation warehouse task is created to move the Pallet from Staging bin to Deconsolidation workstation. Unloading tasks are not created as per changed setup.

Conclusion –

Decoupling Unload and GR with POSC setup where TU is used for Inbound operations can be achieved by disregarding Unloading warehouse steps from POSC and setting up goods movement to TU.

This approach enables us to handle Missing pallets/extra pallets in the IBD with very ease for warehouse employees. Reversing GR and ‘reverse unloading ( without WT ) can be executed at IBD level.

Thanks for reading the blog, I hope that this can be beneficial.

Thank you,

Harish Patil

entry.title: Freight Order Quantity Analysis
entry.content:
In SAP Transportation Management in transportation execution and operational visibility scenarios. In one weekly review, the business team wanted to understand whether transportation volume was evenly distributed across carriers.

The first comparison was based mainly on the number of completed Freight Orders. At first glance, two carriers looked almost equal because their Freight Order counts were close. However, when we reviewed the transported gross weight and gross volume, we found that the actual workload was not the same.

For confidentiality, I have used representative carrier names and adjusted figures. The scenario is based on the type of operational review I have worked on, but the numbers are not production values.
Accessing the App the Freight Order Quantity Analysis app can be accessed from the SAP Fiori launchpad.

  1. Open the SAP Fiori launchpad.
  2. Search for Freight Order Quantity Analysis.
  3. Open the app with Fiori app ID F2676.
  4. From the overview page, select the relevant analytical card or data point.
  5. Navigate to the detailed view to analyze the Freight Orders behind the selected KPI.

The overview page provides different analytical cards, such as gross weight, gross volume, quantity distribution, carrier quantity analysis, shipper quantity analysis, ship to party quantity analysis, transportation mode and utilization.


Figure 1: Freight Order Quantity Analysis overview page - Fiori app F2676

Filters Used for the Analysis from the overview page, I selected the Carrier Quantity Analysis card and opened the detailed view. For the analysis, I used the following filter and display selections:

  • Transportation Mode Category: Road
  • Destination Country/Region: United States
  • View By: Carrier
  • Measure: Gross Weight
  • Display Weight Unit: Metric Tons
  • Display Volume Unit: Cubic Metres
  • Display Quantity Unit: Each
  • Completion Period: Selected completion weeks within the available current and previous month period

The standard app focuses on completed Freight Orders for the current and previous month. Because of this, I used the Completion Year/Week field to compare weekly movement trends.

These fields helped to compare the number of Freight Orders against the actual transportation volume.


Figure 2: Detailed view with carrier, transportation mode, units and completion week filters

Representative Carrier Comparison, The original business question was simple: Which carrier handled more work this week?
If we looked only at the number of Freight Orders, Carrier A appeared to have handled slightly more work than Carrier B.

Measure

Carrier A

Carrier B

Completed Freight Orders

42

39

Gross Weight

840 metric tons

1,260 metric tons

Gross Volume

1,960 m³

2,450 m³

Based on Freight Order count alone, Carrier A looked slightly higher because it completed 42 Freight Orders compared with Carrier B’s 39 Freight Orders. However, the quantity analysis showed a different story. Carrier B completed three fewer Freight Orders, but transported approximately 50% more gross weight. Carrier B also transported higher gross volume.
This finding changed the discussion. Carrier A had more documents, but Carrier B had a higher actual transportation workload. Carrier B was handling fewer but heavier movements, while Carrier A was handling a larger number of smaller shipments.

The main finding was that Freight Order count should not be used as the only measure of carrier workload. In this example, the business initially assumed that Carrier A had a slightly higher workload because it had more completed Freight Orders. After reviewing gross weight and gross volume, we could see that Carrier B had consumed more transportation capacity.
This was useful for three reasons:
1. It gave the team a better view of actual carrier workload.
2. It supported a more meaningful capacity discussion with carriers.
3. It helped explain why carrier utilization may look different from the document count.

The app also helped identify weekly movement patterns. When I changed the view to Completion Week, I could see the week in which Carrier B’s transported weight increased. This made it easier to review the underlying Freight Orders and understand what caused the change. Drilling Down into the Freight Orders after identifying the increase for Carrier B, I reviewed the detailed table and checked the Freight Orders behind the result.
Compared the following information:

  • Source and destination locations
  • Gross weight and volume
  • Number of containers
  • Transportation mode
  • Freight Order completion date
  • Carrier assignment

The list showed that Carrier B had handled several high weight movements to the same destination region during that week. This explained why the gross weight was higher even though the Freight Order count was lower. This is the part I found most useful. The overview helped identify the trend and the detailed view helped explain the reason behind it.


Figure 3: Carrier comparison showing Freight Order count, gross weight, and gross volume

It is also important to understand that this analysis is based on completed Freight Orders. Freight Orders that are still being planned or executed may not be represented in the same way. This can explain differences between the application and another operational report that includes planned or in execution documents.
From a technical perspective, the app uses the C_FrtOrdQuantityQuery CDS view. The C_FRTORDQUANTITYQUERY_CDS OData service must be activated in the SAP Gateway system. Users also need the required SAP Transportation Management and SAP Fiori authorizations to access the app and view the analytical data. No custom development was required for this analysis. The key requirements were correct Freight Order data, the required authorizations and activation of the supporting analytical service.

Conclusion
The Freight Order Quantity Analysis app gives better insight into transportation volumes and supports more informed decision making. It helps teams understand not just how many Freight Orders are moving, but also how much business volume is being executed. The app may look simple at first, but when used together with operational knowledge, it can provide useful insights that are not always visible from standard Freight Order reports.

Reference: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/612fbe25aed64068a7aca8e8e8339de1.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com

entry.title: SAP Document AI Trial Booster Fails at "Subscribing SaaS Applications"
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

 
I am trying to set up SAP Document AI Trial in an SAP BTP Trial subaccount using the "Set Up Account for SAP Document AI" Booster.
 
Environment:
- SAP BTP Trial Account
- Region: US10
- Subaccount: 9e0da020trial
- Runtime: Cloud Foundry
 
Booster Result:
The booster consistently completes the following steps successfully:
✓ Assigning Service Quotas
✓ Creating Service Instances
 
However, it fails at:
 
✗ Subscribing SaaS Applications
 
As a result:
 
↷ Assigning Role Collections is skipped
 
Current Status:
 
The following service instance was created successfully:
 
Service: SAP Document AI Trial
Technical Name: document-information-extraction-trial
Instance: default_document-information-extraction-trial
Plan: blocks_of_100
 
Available Role Templates:
 
Under Security → Roles, I can see the following Document AI role templates:
 
- Document_Information_Extraction_UI_End_User_trial
- Document_Information_Extraction_UI_Document_Viewer_trial
- Document_Information_Extraction_UI_Templates_Admin_trial
- Document_Information_Extraction_Attr_Dox_Client_Id_trial
 
Manual Actions Already Performed:
 
1. Created a custom role collection.
2. Added all available Document AI roles to the role collection.
3. Assigned the role collection to my user.
4. Logged out and logged back in.
 
Result:
 
When I open the tenant UI URL, I receive:
 
"401 Sorry, you are not authorized to use this application with tenant 9e0da020trial."
 
Additional Findings:
 
1. Under Services → Instances and Subscriptions → Subscriptions, I only see:
- SAP Build Code
- SAP HANA Cloud
- SAP Business Application Studio
- SAP Build Work Zone
 
SAP Document AI Trial Basic UI is NOT present.
 
2. Under Security → Trust Configuration → Establish Trust, I get:
"No tenants available"
 
3. In Service Marketplace, SAP Document AI Trial is available and service instances can be created, but there is no:
- Subscribe
- Create Subscription
- Go to Application
option available.
 
Questions:
 
1. Is the missing SAP Document AI Trial Basic UI subscription the reason for the 401 error?
2. Can the UI subscription be created manually after the booster has failed?
3. Is this a known limitation or issue in SAP BTP Trial accounts?
4. Does SAP Document AI Trial UI require IAS/OIDC trust even when the service instance, service key and roles already exist?
5. Has anyone successfully resolved this without modifying the subaccount identity provider configuration?
 
Any guidance or workaround would be greatly

entry.title: Re: API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 2: API Governance with Developer Hub
entry.content:
We are currently evaluating the transition from Classic API Management to the new API-Centric Integration capabilities running on the Integration Cell runtime.
It looks like classic API proxies cannot be deployed directly to the Integration Cell and must be manually recreated as the new API Artifact format.
Could the product team or community clarify:
  1. Is there an official migration tool or utility to convert classic proxies to the new API Artifact format?
  2. If manual recreation is the only option, are there any best practices or tools to speed up this process?
  3. What is the roadmap for automated migration support?

entry.title: Re: SO, DO , Billing Status on Document Flow
entry.content:
Can you confirm that you have already verified

  • that the “Update Document Flow” option is set to “X” in the copy control settings for VTAA (order/order), VTLA (order/delivery), and VTAF (invoice/credit memo)?
  • Also verify the accuracy of the quantity settings and other relevant parameters in the copy control related to billing (VTFF).
  • No relevant OSS notes available for your exact version

If the status is correct on each document, you could improve the display of the document flow.

  • 2449464 - How to enhance the SD Document Flow

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: AI in your Business, SAP UI5 Survey, July Challenges and Summer Events!
entry.content:
Featured Blog Posts 

Integrating SAP Concur with SAP IAG 

Integrating SAP IAG Enterprise Edition with SAP Concur goes beyond the standard setup guides, and this practical blog post by SAP Community member Swapnil Balharpure helps bridge that gap by sharing valuable implementation experience from a real project. The guide walks through the complete integration process, from the initial SAP IAG setup and SAP Concur OAuth configuration to user provisioning, repository synchronization, access risk analysis, and user ID mapping. Along the way, Swapnil highlights important technical considerations, common implementation challenges, mandatory custom fields, provisioning behavior, and current product limitations, offering practical recommendations to help consultants avoid common pitfalls. If you are planning or currently working on an SAP IAG and SAP Concur integration, this blog post is a must read.  

Read Swapnil Blog Post here 

What Partners need to know about the SAP Business AI Platform 

The SAP expert Yella Reddy explains one of the latest changes to the SAP Partner competency framework: since June 30, 2026, the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) competency has officially been renamed to the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) competency. While there are no changes to the current competency requirements and no action is required from existing partners, the blog explains why this represents much more than a simple name change. Yella explores how this update reflects SAP's AI-first strategy, what it means for partner positioning, and why organizations should begin aligning their offerings around AI-driven business outcomes, intelligent automation, SAP Joule Studio, and agentic AI use cases. The post also outlines the next milestones planned for October 2026 and January 2027, when the competency framework will begin introducing new specialization structures and updated knowledge requirements. As the author highlights, "the transition from BTP to BAIP is not simply a branding update — it is an early indicator of how SAP is redefining platform value in the era of Business AI." If you are part of the SAP Partner ecosystem, this is an announcement you will not want to miss. 

Read Yella Reddy Blog Post here 

From Coffee Sales to Business Predictions with SAP RPT 

What if forecasting daily sales could be done without training a machine learning model or deploying complex AI infrastructure? In this hands-on blog post, SAP Champion Chandra Bhushan Singh demonstrates how to use SAP RPT, SAP's foundation model for tabular data, together with SAP AI Core, to predict daily coffee shop sales. Using a simple coffee shop scenario, Chandra walks through the complete process—from setting up SAP AI Core and AI Launchpad to building a Jupyter Notebook, preparing historical data, calling the RPT API, and interpreting the predictions. While the example focuses on coffee sales, the same approach can be applied to demand planning, sales forecasting, customer churn, fraud detection, equipment failure prediction, and many other business scenarios. Throughout the guide, he combines technical implementation with a practical business example, and at the end of the blog invites the community to share feedback, suggestions, and ideas for future SAP AI topics. 

Read Chandra Bhushan Blog Post here 

SAP S/4HANA Custom ABAP CDS View to Datasphere 

If you are working with SAP Datasphere data integration, SAP expert Jeet Kapase shares a comprehensive guide on how to replicate data from a custom ABAP CDS view in SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere using Replication Flows with Change Data Capture (CDC). The blog explains the end-to-end setup, including how RMS, APE, and CDC work together, how to prepare a CDC-enabled custom CDS view, configure Replication Flows for Initial Only and Initial + Delta loads, and understand how deltas are captured through logging tables, buffer tables, and background jobs in SAP S/4HANA. Jeet also demonstrates how to validate inserts, updates, and deletes end to end, explains the different Change_Type values used in SAP Datasphere, and shares monitoring guidance, tuning recommendations, and best practices to help teams build reliable CDC-based replication scenarios. 

Read Jeet Kapase Blog Post here 

Community News 

July Developer Challenge  

We are already in Week 2 of the July SAP Developer Challenge, and we are excited to see so many community members actively participating during the first week! This month's four-week challenge invites developers to build a multiplayer Snake & Ladder game using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) and CAP Status Transition Flows (@flow.status). Throughout the month, participants will progressively build a complete CAP service featuring turn management, dice rolls, snake and ladder logic, SAP Fiori integration, and even support for the CAP MCP Server, allowing AI assistants to interact with the game through natural language. Every week, a new blog post introduces the next challenge, together with implementation guidance and hints, and participants simply submit their solutions as comments on that week's post. The challenge runs throughout July, with Week 2 (July 13–19) focusing on Roll & Move, followed by Multiplayer & Events (July 20–26) and Fiori UI & CAP MCP (July 27–31). If you would like to join, simply follow the weekly blog posts on SAP Community, complete each task, and submit your answer in the comments to participate alongside the community.  

Read the full blog post here for more information on how to participate!  

Ask Me Anything with SAP Champion: July Edition 

As we' have highlighted in the previous edition, SAP Champion Martin Pankraz is taking the lead for this month Ask Me Anything with the SAP Champions series! And we would like to friendly remind you to take this chance to take part and send your questions. Martin is an expert in SAP Security topics, and this is your chance to understand better about securing SAP S/4HANA, RISE, GROW, SAP BTP, identity and access management, integration security, threat detection, or even the emerging challenges around AI agents accessing SAP data. Questions can be submitted anonymously through Microsoft Forms (link here) and in the comments of Martin blog post. Make sure to keep an eye on the SAP Community YouTube channel for Martin's video answering all your questions! 

SAPUI5 Survey: We want to hear from you! 

As AI continues to reshape how enterprise applications are built, used, and experienced, SAP wants to hear directly from the community. There are only a few days left to participate in the SAP UI Technology Strategy Survey (participate here), which remains open until July 19, 2026. In just five minutes, you can share your perspective on topics such as how well SAPUI5 meets your needs today and in the age of AI, the UI experiences you expect to dominate enterprise applications in the coming years, the capabilities that matter most for your future development work, and where you see SAP's UI technology strategy heading. Whether you are a SAPUI5/Fiori developer, UI or UX architect, technical lead, enterprise architect, or IT decision maker, this is your opportunity to help influence the future direction of SAP UI technologies. Make sure to complete the survey before July 19 and let your voice be heard! More details about it here.  

Events 

SAP Stammtisch Bamberg  

If you are in the Bamberg region and looking to connect with your local SAP community, do not miss the next SAP Stammtisch Bamberg on July 30. Formerly known as Stammtisch Oberfranken, this informal meetup is an opportunity to exchange ideas, discuss the latest SAP topics, and network with fellow SAP professionals in a relaxed atmosphere. The community will meet from 6:00 PM at St. GeorgenBräu Bierkeller in Buttenheim, and even if you cannot make it right at the start, you're welcome to join later in the evening. Do not forget to join the Bamberg Location Group to stay informed about future meetups and community activities! Register here. 

Online and Free Event: From Classical SAP to AI Architect 

Looking to explore how Generative AI and SAP BTP are transforming the SAP landscape? The SAP community Nigeria is hosting a virtual session on July 22 at 3:00 PM (WAT) titled "From Classical SAP to AI Architect: Solving Business Bottlenecks with Generative AI and the Adaptive Semantic Extraction Framework on SAP BTP for S/4HANA." Whether you are a classical SAP professional beginning your AI journey or looking to deepen your SAP BTP knowledge, this session will explore what AI and BTP mean for traditional SAP modules and how you can start expanding your skill set. The event will also feature a real-world success story demonstrating how the Adaptive Semantic Extraction Framework was used to automate complex sales orders and solve major operational bottlenecks. The session is free and virtual, so make sure to reserve your spot on the official event page here.  

SAP Inside Track´s in July!

As we mentioned in our last edition, this month, we are excited to have two major SAP Inside Track events taking place! We would like to remind everyone in United States and Brazil, that you still have time to sign up for both SAP Inside Track Columbus Registration and SAP Inside Track Sao Paulo Registration.  

More information: 

On July 25, the first-ever SAP Inside Track Columbus will bring the local SAP community together in Columbus, Ohio, at 200 E Campus View Blvd, Suite 200, for a full day of learning and networking. The event is designed for everyone interested in SAP. Alongside a full day of expert-led sessions, attendees will have the opportunity to connect with fellow SAP professionals from across the Midwest over food, drinks, and discussions. 

The following day, on July 26, SAP Inside Track São Paulo will take place at PUC Marques de Paranaguá – Auditório Marques de Paranaguá, in São Paulo, Brazil. This community-driven event welcomes customers, consultants, developers, architects, and freelancers for a full day of technical sessions presented in Portuguese and English. As with every SAP Inside Track, attendees are encouraged to help shape the agenda and contribute to the community experience.

entry.title: Re: ABAP CDS Annotation for Hierarchy Fiori Elements UI a...
entry.content: SAP definitly advises against implementing hierarchies by means of the unmanaged query! It is extremely complex and needs detailed knowledge on internal implicit (and meanwhile deprecated, non-supported) contracts. Therefore SAP strongly advises to consider an upgrade to the OP 2025 release and using the provided functionality for OData V4.

entry.title: 5 Fiori Apps Every Sales Key User Should Know in the O2C (SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud)
entry.content:
The Order-to-Cash (O2C) process is at the heart of Sales. From order creation to delivery and billing, sales teams need transparency, efficiency, and fast access to relevant information. SAP Fiori provides a range of role-based applications that help users manage and monitor the entire process more effectively.

Here are five Fiori apps that can significantly improve productivity across the O2C process.

My Sales Overview (App ID F2200)

My Sales Overview provides a centralized dashboard for internal sales representatives. Using actionable cards, users can quickly access open sales orders, quotations, customer returns, and other key sales information. It serves as an ideal starting point for monitoring daily sales activities.

SAP Help:
My Sales Overview | SAP Help Portal

Manage Sales Orders (App ID F1873)

Manage Sales Orders is one of the most important applications in the O2C process. It allows users to search, filter, create, and manage sales orders while providing visibility into the status of each order and its related processes.

Key benefits include:

  • Fast access to customer orders
  • Flexible filtering and search capabilities
  • Easy navigation to order details and document flow
  • Improved order management efficiency

SAP Help:
Manage Sales Orders | SAP Help Portal

Track Sales Orders (App ID F2577)

Customers increasingly expect real-time updates on their orders. Track Sales Orders enables sales representatives to monitor the fulfillment status of customer orders and quickly identify whether orders have been delivered, invoiced, or are still being processed.

Key benefits include:

  • End-to-end visibility of order status
  • Access to document flow information
  • Faster customer response times
  • Improved order tracking capabilities

SAP Help:
Track Sales Orders | SAP Help Portal

Sales Order Fulfillment – Analyze and Resolve Issues (App ID F0029A)

This app helps sales teams proactively identify and resolve issues that could delay order fulfillment. Examples include credit blocks, missing information, delivery issues, or invoicing problems. It provides a comprehensive view of fulfillment-related bottlenecks and supports faster issue resolution.

Key benefits include:

  • Early identification of fulfillment risks
  • Faster issue resolution
  • Improved customer satisfaction
  • Enhanced visibility across the O2C process

SAP Help:
Sales Order Fulfillment - Analyze and Resolve Issues | SAP Help Portal

Manage Billing Documents (App ID F0797A)

The billing process represents the final step in the O2C cycle. Manage Billing Documents enables users to manage invoices, credit memos, and other billing documents from a central location. Users can display, edit, post, and cancel billing documents while maintaining full transparency throughout the billing process.

Manage Billing Documents – Version 2 (F0797A) is the successor to the classic app and offers an improved user experience as well as additional capabilities for managing billing documents more efficiently.

Key benefits include:

  • Centralized billing document management
  • Enhanced transparency in the billing process
  • Faster processing of billing-related activities
  • Support for efficient revenue realization

SAP Help:

Manage Billing Documents - Version 2 | SAP Help Portal

Conclusion

SAP Fiori provides powerful applications that support users throughout the entire Order-to-Cash process. The combination of My Sales Overview (App ID F2200), Manage Sales Orders (App ID F1873), Track Sales Orders (App ID F2577), Sales Order Fulfillment – Analyze and Resolve Issues (App ID F0029A) and Manage Billing Documents (App ID F0797A) helps sales teams improve transparency, increase efficiency, and deliver better customer service.

entry.title: Re: Enable Sorting of Outer Dimension by Measure in Plann...
entry.content:
Hi @Sim00n ,
You've identified a real platform constraint. Beyond the design alternatives, there is a script-based workaround you could explore.

Instead of relying on the UI sort/rank features, you can use a script to programmatically sort the table when the page loads. The script would reorder the Customer dimension by Quantity (highest first), while Version blocks stay in their fixed order underneath each Customer.

After the sort is applied, you restore the table's input-readiness so users can still plan data.

I hope this idea helps you.

entry.title: Re: Difference between SAC Enterprise and SAC Embedded (K...
entry.content:
Hi @vlafosse ,

Ah, that's a completely different thing than what I described earlier. Let me clarify.

The "Manage KPI and Reports" tile in S/4HANA Cloud is native, built-in analytics, not SAC Embedded Edition on BTP

When your customer uses that tile, they're accessing S/4HANA's embedded analytics, where SAP Analytics Cloud serves as the underlying engine powering the Stories tab specifically, but users never log into a separate SAC system, all analytical reports are accessible directly through SAP Fiori apps in S/4HANA Cloud.

Key difference:

S/4HANA Embedded Analytics (what the customer is using):

  • Built-in, included with S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, zero additional license cost
  • Runs on the same SAP HANA database as the transactional system (no data replication needed)
  • Reports are live, real-time, built via SAP Fiori analytical apps and the Manage KPIs and Reports app
  • Surfaces operational reports and KPIs through the Fiori Launchpad
  • Limited to S/4HANA data via CDS (Core Data Services) views
  • No separate login, everything is within S/4HANA
  • Predefined content covers finance, procurement, supply chain (500+ Fiori analytical apps from SAP)

SACEE on BTP (deprecated anyway, but for context):

  • Separate, paid service on BTP, needed to be provisioned separately from S/4HANA
  • Live-HANA-only connectivity, max 150 concurrent users, no planning/predictive, API-only admin
  • Removed from Discovery Center March 15, 2025, not available for new deployments

So the "Manage KPI and Reports" tile is not SACEE it's the native S/4HANA embedded analytics capability, and it uses SAC's Stories engine under the hood for the Stories tab only. No connection to the deprecated SACEE product.

About the Publish to Catalog question: If the customer creates a custom story and wants to share it from that tile, they'd use standard S/4HANA authorization controls, not a separate "Publish to Catalog" feature. The tile's own sharing/visibility is controlled via Fiori roles and business catalogs.

Does that clear up the distinction?

entry.title: MTE: SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS/IPS): Open Office Hours for SAP SuccessFactors HCM

In this session, customers will have the opportunity to get answers to their questions about the migration process, key considerations, and best practices to ensure a smooth transition from legacy authentication methods to SAP’s recommended approach.
