entry.title: Your SAP Commerce Cloud Data, On Your Terms: Introducing SQL View Gateway
entry.content:
If you've ever needed to get data out of SAP Commerce Cloud for a downstream integration, a report, or a custom data pipeline, you already know the challenge. The data is there — orders, customers, products, transactions — but getting it out reliably, securely, and without building something from scratch has not always been straightforward.

SQL View Gateway, available in SAP Commerce Cloud since April 2026, is a new answer to that challenge.

How teams have been solving this until now

There is no shortage of ways to extract data from SAP Commerce Cloud. Teams today use a range of approaches depending on their context:

  • Custom integrations — partner-built or developed in-house, tailored to specific needs
  • DBSync booster — replicates Commerce data to a secondary database for downstream consumption
  • ImpEx file export — extracts data in structured files for batch processing
  • OData API — exposes Commerce data through a standardized API layer

Each of these is a legitimate solution. They serve different needs, and many customers use them successfully. But they share a common constraint: none of them provide direct, real-time, read-only SQL access to Commerce data without additional infrastructure, custom development, or both. For teams that need exactly that — a direct SQL connection, minimal overhead, and data as it exists right now — there was no supported out-of-the-box option.

SQL View Gateway fills that gap.

What SQL View Gateway is

SQL View Gateway gives you direct, read-only SQL access to your SAP Commerce Cloud data using any standard SQL client, BI tool, or data pipeline you already work with.

There is no file to export, no secondary database to maintain, and no custom API to build. You connect using standard SQL credentials and query the data directly — the same way you would connect to any database.

What data can you access?

SQL View Gateway exposes SAP Commerce data as SQL views. In practice, this means you can query business objects like:

  • Orders and order entries — status, totals, discounts, delivery details
  • Products and catalog data — product codes, names, categories, pricing
  • Customers — account data, registration details
  • Returns and refunds — return requests, refund amounts, statuses
  • Promotions and discounts — applied discounts, vouchers
  • And more — any Commerce type that your team configures for exposure

You decide exactly which types and attributes each consumer can see, through a role configuration defined in your project code. Security-critical data — passwords, tokens, API credentials, access restrictions, and internal framework types — is automatically blocked before it can ever be exposed, regardless of what you configure. The block list is maintained by SAP and applied at the platform level.

It is worth being explicit about one thing: SQL View Gateway does not manage data privacy compliance on your behalf. The data that comes through is raw Commerce data. You are responsible for ensuring that what you expose — and to whom — is compliant with applicable regulations, including data processing obligations, consent requirements , and cross-border data transfer rules. This is no different from any other data integration approach, but it is important to factor in when designing your roles and deciding what to share with downstream systems.

How it works technically

SQL View Gateway runs on a dedicated compute layer that is completely separate from your primary Commerce database. This matters for two reasons:

  • No performance impact on your production system. Queries against the gateway run on their own compute resources. Your store-front transactions are unaffected no matter how heavy the load on the gateway side.
  • Near real-time data. The gateway accesses the same underlying data as your production system without going through a separate replication process. Under normal conditions, the data you query reflects your live Commerce state with millisecond-level latency.

The gateway enforces TLS 1.2 or higher on all connections. Access is read-only by design — it is not possible to write to Commerce data through SQL View Gateway.

From concept to connection: how setup works

Setting up SQL View Gateway involves four steps. Two are handled by developers in the Commerce project, two are handled by administrators in Cloud Portal.

Step 1 — Define roles (developer)
A role specifies which Commerce types and attributes a particular consumer is allowed to see. Roles are defined as configuration files in the data-extraction-configuration directory of your Commerce project — plain text, version-controlled alongside your code.

A role called salesreporting, for example, might expose order totals, statuses, and customer references, but not payment details or personal contact data. You can define as many roles as you need.

Step 2 — Deploy (developer)
Commit the role configuration and trigger a standard Commerce deployment. The system reads the role definitions and creates the corresponding SQL views in the gateway database. No special deployment steps are needed beyond selecting the correct update mode.

Step 3 — Provision a gateway (administrator)
In Cloud Portal, create a gateway for the target environment. You select the compute tier (which determines cost and throughput), configure network access (VPN or public), and specify the IP range of the machines that will be allowed to connect — this is a security measure that restricts access to only the specific systems you designate as consumers. Provisioning takes approximately 20 minutes. You can create up to 20 gateways per environment — useful if different teams or systems need separate connectivity with different performance characteristics.

Step 4 — Create credentials (administrator)
Create a named credential in Cloud Portal, assign it to one of your deployed roles, and the system generates a login and password. Share those credentials with the downstream system or team. Each credential can only access the data scope of its assigned role — nothing more.

That's it. The downstream tool connects via standard SQL using the generated credentials, and queries the views defined by the role.

Cost: what to expect

SQL View Gateway is billed through Consumption Credits — SAP's flexible pay-per-use model. The cost of a gateway depends on its compute tier, measured in Capacity Units per month.

Gateway compute is sized in vCores: 2, 4, 8, up to 32. There is a minimum constraint — a gateway must be at least 25% of your primary database's vCores. A small Commerce environment on 8 primary vCores would need at least a 2 vCore gateway. A larger environment on 32 primary vCores would start at a minimum of 8 vCores.

The right sizing depends on your workload: how many consumers connect simultaneously, how frequently they query, and how complex those queries are. SAP provides a Capacity Estimator and a resource planning guide to help you work through this. A practical approach is to start at the minimum allowed tier and scale up if CPU utilization consistently approaches 80%.

If you do not yet have Consumption Credits, this is a good moment to consider making the switch from your current billing model. Consumption Credits give you fine-grained cost visibility and the flexibility to scale usage up or down without renegotiating your contract.

SQL View Gateway and Business Data Cloud: understanding the difference

SAP offers two complementary solutions for working with Commerce data beyond the store-front: SQL View Gateway and Business Data Cloud. They are not competing products. They solve different problems, and understanding which one to reach for matters.

Business Data Cloud is SAP's strategic platform for enterprise analytics. It brings together data from multiple SAP sources — Commerce, S/4HANA, Ariba, and others — and delivers it as standardized, harmonized data products. These are curated, semantically rich datasets ready for business intelligence reporting, advanced analytics, and machine learning. If your goal is a unified view of your business across your SAP landscape, with the governance and AI capabilities to match, Business Data Cloud is the right tool .

SQL View Gateway works at a different level. It gives you direct access to raw, live Commerce data — exactly as it exists in the database at that moment, structured the way your Commerce platform structures it. There is no aggregation, no harmonization across sources, no semantic model applied. What you get is fast, lightweight, and flexible: a direct SQL connection to your Commerce data with the access controls you define. SQL View Gateway is the right choice when you need that raw connection — to power a custom pipeline, synchronize a specific dataset to an external system, build an operational dashboard on live data, or support a use case where the data model is still evolving and you need the flexibility to work with it directly.

To make it concrete: if a logistics team wants a live view of order statuses and consignment data to monitor SLA compliance, SQL View Gateway is the direct path. If the business wants a cross-brand performance dashboard combining Commerce revenue data with finance data from S/4HANA, Business Data Cloud is the right starting point.

The two solutions serve different purposes and different audiences. Choosing between them is not an either/or decision — your organization may have good reasons to use both, for different use cases.

 SQL View GatewayBusiness Data Cloud
DataRaw, live Commerce dataHarmonized, multi-source data products
LatencyMilliseconds (real-time)Near real-time to batch, depending on pipeline
ScopeSAP Commerce CloudMultiple SAP sources combined
Best forDirect access, custom pipelines, operational dataBI, advanced analytics, ML, AI insights
SetupLightweight, self-service in Cloud PortalBroader platform setup

When in doubt: if your question is "I need to get live Commerce data into my system," SQL View Gateway is likely what you need. If your question is "I need analytics across my SAP landscape," Business Data Cloud is the right starting point.

A few things to plan for

SQL View Gateway is designed to be self-service and straightforward to set up, but a few operational considerations are worth knowing upfront:

  • Hyperscale tier required. SQL View Gateway is only available on the Hyperscale database tier. If you are on a different database tier, it is not currently available. Your SAP account team can confirm your current tier.
  • Gateway availability. SQL View Gateway is not classified as a business-critical service. During maintenance, system initialization, or database backup operations, the gateway enters a temporarily blocked state. Consumers should be designed to handle brief unavailability gracefully rather than treating the connection as always-on.
  • One role per credential. Each credential gives access to exactly one role's data scope. If a downstream system needs data from multiple roles, it will need multiple credentials and connections. This is a deliberate security design — it keeps access auditable and enforces least privilege.
  • All access is logged. Every connection event and every credential management action is recorded in an audit trail. This is useful for compliance and for tracking down unexpected access patterns.

Who should consider SQL View Gateway

SQL View Gateway is most relevant if you recognize any of these situations:

  • You need a live data connection from Commerce to an external system — a data warehouse, an operational dashboard, a custom pipeline — without building a bespoke extract.
  • Your existing data access approach is not the right fit for a specific use case. SQL View Gateway is not a replacement for the other approaches; it is an additional option for scenarios they do not cover well.
  • You are migrating from on-premise Commerce to the cloud. Direct database access was common on-prem. SQL View Gateway is the supported, governed equivalent i n the cloud.
  • You want to connect Commerce data to SAP Datasphere or SAP Analytics Cloud. SQL View Gateway provides a convenient SQL data source for this integration. We will cover this end-to-end in the third post in this series.
  • You need auditable, role-scoped data access. Each connection sees only what its role permits, and every access event is logged.

What you need to get started

SQL View Gateway is available to all SAP Commerce Cloud customers running on the Hyperscale database tier. If you're not sure which tier you're on, your SAP account team can confirm.

You also need Consumption Credits. Once both prerequisites are in place, provisioning a gateway is a self-service operation in Cloud Portal — no support ticket required.

What's next in this series

This post covers the what and the why. The next two posts go deeper:

  • Post 2 — Technical deep-dive: Architecture, role configuration in detail, gateway provisioning, credential management, connectivity options, and cost estimation walk-through.
  • Post 3 — Integration guide: Step-by-step walk-through of connecting SAP Commerce Cloud to SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud using SQL View Gateway.

Learn more

  • Business overview video
  • Configuration guide video
  • Roles and data scopes guide video
  • Resource planning and estimating guide video
  • Capacity Estimator documentation
  • Data extraction documentation
  • SQL View Gateway documentation 

Have questions or want to share how you're planning to use SQL View Gateway? Drop a comment below.

entry.title: SQL View Gateway: A Technical Guide to Setup and Configuration
entry.content:
SQL View Gateway: A Technical Guide to Setup and Configuration

The first post in this series covered what SQL View Gateway is and why it exists. This post goes deeper: how the pieces fit together, and how to configure everything end to end.

Setting up SQL View Gateway involves four steps. Two are done by a developer in the Commerce project. Two are done by an administrator in Cloud Portal. They don't need to happen at the same time, and Steps 1–2 and Step 3 can proceed independently — an administrator can provision a gateway before any roles are deployed. The only hard dependency is at the last step: a credential cannot be created without an existing, deployed role to assign to it.

How SQL View Gateway works

Before diving into configuration, it helps to understand what you're actually setting up.

SQL View Gateway runs on a dedicated compute layer that is completely separate from your primary Commerce database. Queries from external tools run on that separate layer — your storefront transactions are unaffected no matter how heavy the load on the gateway side.

The gateway accesses the same underlying data as your production system. Under normal conditions, the data you query reflects your live Commerce state with millisecond-level latency. There is no separate replication process, no scheduled export, no intermediate file.

Access is read-only by design. It is not possible to write to Commerce data through SQL View Gateway. All connections are required to use TLS 1.2 or higher. Standard SQL drivers and clients honor this requirement by default.

The data is exposed as SQL views. Each view corresponds to a Commerce type — orders, products, customers, and so on — with exactly the attributes your configuration exposes. Nothing more.

Step 1: Define roles (developer)

A role specifies which Commerce types and attributes a particular consumer is allowed to see. Roles are the foundation of everything else — a gateway credential without a deployed role has nothing to expose.

Where role configuration lives

Role definitions are stored in .properties files inside a data-extraction-configuration directory within any extension's resources folder. You can spread role configuration across multiple extensions — the system collects all .properties files from all data-extraction-configuration directories it finds and applies the combined result.

How you organize this is up to you. Some teams keep all roles in one file in one extension. Others split by role, by domain, or keep role definitions in the extension that introduces the relevant Commerce types. Subdirectories within data-extraction-configuration are fine too — the system scans recursively, so folder structure doesn't matter. Any structure works — the system doesn't care about file names or how many files there are, only about the properties they contain.

The only requirement: the extensions containing your role configuration must be included in your project via manifest.json or localextensions.xml. No special entry for data-extraction-configuration is needed — standard extension inclusion is sufficient.

This is plain text, version-controlled alongside your code. Treat it like any other configuration — peer review, change history, deployment pipeline.

The properties format

Each role requires a declaration of which types to expose, and optionally a list of attributes for each type:

data-extraction.role-based-config.<roleName>.types=<Type1>,<Type2>
data-extraction.role-based-config.<roleName>.<Type>.attributes=<attr1>,<attr2>,<attr3>

The attribute list is optional. If you declare only the type, the system exposes all attributes of that type that exist in the database and are not on the security block list. This is a convenient shortcut for types where you want broad access, but be deliberate — for types like Customer or User, "all attributes" may include more than you intend.

If you declare attributes without a matching type declaration, they are silently skipped.

A worked example: salesreporting

Say you want to give your reporting team access to order data — totals, statuses, discounts — but not payment details or customer contact information.

data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.types=Order,OrderEntry,ReturnRequest,Discount,Product
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Order.attributes=PK,guid,code,creationtime,modifiedtime,currency,date,status,totalPrice,totalTax,totalDiscountsAmount,deliveryCost,net
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.OrderEntry.attributes=PK,creationtime,modifiedtime,entryNumber,order,product,quantity,basePrice,totalPrice,discountValues
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.ReturnRequest.attributes=PK,code,order,creationtime,modifiedtime,status,refundAmount
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Discount.attributes=PK,code,value,type,currency,creationtime
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Product.attributes=PK,code,name,description

This gives the reporting team a focused, minimal view of what they need — without exposing anything they don't.

Localized attributes

Some Commerce types have localized attributes — attributes that hold different values per language, such as Product.name or Product.description. SQL View Gateway handles these differently from regular attributes, and the behavior depends on how you configure them.

Implicit locale — write the attribute name without a language qualifier:

data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.types=Order,OrderEntry,ReturnRequest,Discount,Product
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Product.attributes=PK,code,name,description

The system detects that name and description are localized for Product and automatically fetches them in the default language. The view columns are named name and description — no language suffix. The default language is en, configurable via a system property in local.properties:

data-extraction.view.localized.default-language=en

This is a standard SAP Commerce Cloud system property — not part of the role configuration files.

Explicit locale — append the language in square brackets:

data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Product.attributes=PK,code,name[en],name[pl],description[en]

Each language produces a separate column with a language suffix: name_en, name_pl, description_en. Use this when downstream consumers need to know which language a column contains, or when you need multiple languages side by side.

All-attributes mode (no attribute list) — localized attributes are treated as implicit locale: fetched in the default language, column named without suffix.

Configuration View columnUse case
name[en]name_enMultiple languages side by side; consumer needs to know the language
namenameSingle language; simpler column contract for consumers
No attribute listnameSame as implicit locale

A few rules to be aware of:

  • If the configured default language does not exist in the Commerce system, the attribute is skipped and a warning is logged — no error, no deployment failure
  • Malformed language qualifiers (name[], name[invalid], name[en][de]) are silently filtered out
  • If an item has no translation for the requested language, the localized column contains NULL — the row is still present in the view. If your query must return only items with a translated value, add an IS NOT NULL condition on the localized column

Role name constraints

Role names are validated against a strict pattern:

  • Must start with a lowercase letter
  • Only lowercase letters, digits, and underscores allowed
  • Minimum 3 characters, maximum 30 characters

If a role name violates these rules, it is silently skipped with a warning in the deployment log. No error is thrown, and no deployment fails — which means an invalid role name can go unnoticed if you don't review logs.

What gets blocked automatically

SQL View Gateway maintains a hardcoded block list of security-critical Commerce types and attributes — passwords, tokens, API credentials, access restrictions, internal framework types. These are blocked regardless of what you configure. If you include a blocked attribute in your .properties file, it will simply not appear in the view. No error, no deployment failure.

This is a safety net, not a substitute for good configuration. You remain responsible for what you expose. Notably: SQL View Gateway does not manage data privacy compliance on your behalf. PII — customer names, email addresses, addresses — is not on the block list. If your role exposes it, it will be accessible to anyone with a credential assigned to that role. Design your roles with that in mind.

Multiple roles

You can define as many roles as you need. Each role becomes independently assignable to credentials later.

Plan your roles upfront

Roles and their views are created at deployment time, but they expose no data until a credential is assigned to them. This means you can define a broader set of roles during your initial deployment — for example, separate roles for sales reporting, logistics, and content management — without exposing anything until you deliberately create a credential for each one. When a consumer's needs change, you can reassign an existing credential to a different role in Cloud Portal without any redeployment; the change takes effect within approximately 5–10 minutes.

Step 2: Deploy (developer)

Once your role configuration is in place, commit it and trigger a standard Commerce deployment.

One important note for first-time setup: when introducing SQL View Gateway roles for the first time in an environment, you must select "Recreate (fastest, with downtime)" as the deployment mode. This ensures the necessary database structures are created. After the initial setup, subsequent role updates can be deployed using standard zero-downtime options.

Whenever role definitions change — new types, new attributes, removed roles — always select "Migrate data" under Platform Update Mode. Skipping this means your changes don't take effect.

What the system does during deployment

When the deployment runs, the system reads your role definitions and creates a set of SQL views in the gateway database. Each view is named after the role and type, both lowercased:

salesreporting_order
salesreporting_orderentry
salesreporting_returnrequest
salesreporting_discount
salesreporting_product

The type name in the view corresponds to the Commerce type code in its singular form — Order becomes order, OrderEntry becomes orderentry.

Review deployment logs

After every role deployment, check the deployment logs. Two things can go wrong silently:

  • Typos in attribute names — totalPrize instead of totalPrice is logged as a warning and skipped. The view is created without that attribute.
  • Invalid role names — a role that violates the naming rules is skipped entirely.

Neither causes the deployment to fail. Both cause your views to be incomplete without any obvious signal. Make log review part of your deployment checklist.

Step 3: Provision a gateway (administrator)

With roles deployed, an administrator can provision a gateway in Cloud Portal.

Navigate to: Environments → [your environment] → SQL View Gateways → Create

What you configure at creation time

Name — up to 30 alphanumeric characters, unique within the environment. Choose something that communicates the purpose: reporting-prod, etl-staging, datasphere-prod.

Performance tier — the compute size of the gateway, measured in vCores:

vCoresTypical use
2Development, prototyping, light workloads
4Moderate analytics, occasional queries
8Regular ETL pipelines, multiple consumers
16Heavy analytics, high concurrency
32Very high throughput requirements

One hard constraint: the gateway must have at least 25% of your primary database's vCores. On a primary with 8 vCores, the minimum is 2. On a primary with 32 vCores, the minimum is 8.

The right sizing depends on your workload — query frequency, concurrency, and complexity. A practical starting point: begin at the minimum allowed tier and monitor CPU utilization. If it consistently approaches 80%, move up a tier. SAP provides a Capacity Estimator, a resource planning guide and performance recommendations to help work through this more precisely.

Connectivity mode — VPN or public. Covered in detail below.

IP range — an IP address or CIDR block specifying which machines are allowed to connect. This is a security boundary — only systems that genuinely need access to the gateway should be included. Set at creation; can be updated at any time.

Activation time

Provisioning takes approximately 20 minutes. The gateway moves through states: INACTIVE → ACTIVATING → RUNNING. The gateway is not usable until it reaches RUNNING.

If this is the very first gateway in an environment, there is a one-time environment-level activation step that also takes approximately 20 minutes. Plan for this in first-time setups.

When to use multiple gateways

You can create up to 20 gateways per environment. Multiple gateways make sense when:

  • Different teams or systems need different performance tiers
  • You want network-level separation between consumers (e.g. internal tools vs external partners)
  • You need separate connectivity modes for different use cases
  • You need to protect critical workloads from resource contention — for example, a logistics integration that must remain responsive should not share a gateway with analytics consumers that may run expensive, resource-intensive queries. Separate gateways provide compute isolation between workloads.

If teams just need access to different data scopes, that is handled through roles and credentials — they don't need separate gateways.

Step 4: Create credentials (administrator)

A credential is a named technical account — login and password — that an external tool uses to connect to the gateway. Each credential is assigned to exactly one role, which defines what data it can access.

In Cloud Portal, navigate to the gateway and select Create Credentials:

  1. Enter a name and optional description
  2. Assign a role from your deployed configuration
  3. Confirm — the system generates a login and password

The password is shown exactly once. Copy it immediately and store it securely. There is no way to retrieve it again — if it is lost, you must regenerate it (which invalidates the old one).

One role per credential

Each credential gives access to exactly one role's data scope. If a downstream system needs data from multiple roles — say, both salesreporting and logistics — it needs separate credentials and separate connections. This is intentional: it enforces least privilege and makes audit trails meaningful.

Credential lifecycle

OperationEffect
CreateGenerates login + password; credential becomes active
Regenerate passwordIssues a new password; old password immediately invalid
Change roleAssigns a different role; takes effect within ~5–10 minutes, no deployment required
DeactivateSuspends access without deleting; preserves configuration. Coming soon — not yet available in Cloud Portal.
ReactivateRestores access; option to regenerate password at the same time. Coming soon — not yet available in Cloud Portal.
DeletePermanently removes credential; active sessions are terminated

Rotation without downtime: create a new credential, update the consumer to use it, then delete the old one. The gateway continues serving the old credential until it is explicitly deleted.

Who can manage credentials

Only users with the CUSTOMER_SYS_ADMIN or CUSTOMER_CREDENTIALS_ACCESS role in Cloud Portal can view, create, or manage credentials. The connection string — the endpoint details needed to connect — is only visible to these roles. Developers do not have access to credentials or connection strings.

Connectivity

SQL View Gateway supports two connectivity modes — VPN (recommended) and public access. The mode is selected when creating the gateway and can be changed later by editing the gateway configuration; the change takes approximately 10–20 minutes to apply.

VPN connectivity

How the network is structured on the SAP side

When you create a private SQL View Gateway, it is provisioned inside a dedicated private network on the SAP side. Each Commerce environment gets its own isolated subnet within this network. A firewall is attached to each subnet — this is where your allowed IPs are checked. Only traffic whose source IP matches the allowed IPs you configured in Cloud Portal is permitted through.

Your VPN tunnel terminates at a VPN gateway on the SAP side. From there, traffic is routed directly to the SQL View Gateway subnet — it does not pass through any additional address translation on the SAP side. This means source IP addresses arrive at the firewall unchanged.

What you need to configure on your side

Getting VPN connectivity to SQL View Gateway working requires three things to be in place on your side. The exact configuration will depend on your network architecture — the points below describe what needs to be achieved, with examples of common approaches. Your network specialist will know the right solution for your environment.

1. VPN tunnel

You need a VPN tunnel established to the SAP-side VPN gateway. If you already connect to your Commerce environment via VPN, you are likely using this same tunnel already. No separate tunnel is needed for SQL View Gateway.

2. DNS resolution

The connection URL provided in Cloud Portal contains a DNS hostname. In VPN mode, this hostname is only resolvable within the SAP private network — it will not resolve via public DNS.

The private IP address of the gateway is shown in Cloud Portal alongside the connection string. Your client system needs to be able to resolve the gateway hostname to an IP that your network then routes to the correct destination. Depending on your network setup, this might involve updating a hosts file, configuring an internal DNS server, or another mechanism — your network specialist will know what applies in your environment.

3. Routing and allowed IPs

Two things must align for traffic to reach the gateway:

  • Traffic to the resolved IP must reach the SAP-side VPN gateway through your VPN tunnel. How routing is configured to achieve this depends on your network setup.
  • The source IP of packets as they arrive at the SAP firewall must match the allowed IPs you configured in Cloud Portal. This is the IP as seen after your VPN tunnel — it is not translated on the SAP side. Note that the NAT functionality available as part of SAP Commerce Cloud infrastructure is not involved in SQL View Gateway connectivity — it requires no configuration on your part and has no effect on traffic to or from the gateway. Depending on your network architecture, the source IP could be the IP of the client machine, the IP of your VPN gateway, or the IP of another device in the path. Your network specialist can determine which IP will appear as the source by tracing a test connection. 
Your network setupSource IP seen by SAP firewallWhat to enter in Cloud Portal
No NAT on your VPN gatewayIP of the client machineIP of the client machine
Your VPN gateway performs source NATIP of your VPN gatewayIP of your VPN gateway
Intermediate proxy or load balancerIP of that deviceIP of that device

What to enter in the Cloud Portal "IP range" field

Once you know which IP will appear as the source, enter it in Cloud Portal when creating or editing the gateway. You can enter a single IP address (10.10.5.20) or a CIDR range (10.10.5.0/29) if multiple systems need access.

Enter the narrowest range that covers your use case — this is a security boundary. The maximum block size is /24; larger ranges are rejected.

Typical scenarios

The scenarios below illustrate common configurations. They are not exhaustive — your setup may differ, and your network specialist will be the right person to work out the details.

Scenario A: Client system in a cloud-hosted network

Your BI tool or ETL system runs in a cloud-hosted virtual network connected to Commerce via VPN or network peering. Traffic flows through the SAP VPN gateway to the SQL View Gateway subnet. The source IP is typically the private IP of your virtual machine or service.

  • DNS resolution: your cloud provider likely offers private DNS capabilities that can handle this — your network specialist will know the right approach
  • Routing: your cloud network needs to route traffic to the gateway's private IP through the VPN connection — the specifics depend on your cloud network configuration
  • Allowed IPs: typically the private IP of your machine or service, but verify with your network team

Scenario B: On-premise network, VPN Site-to-Site, no NAT on client VPN gateway

Your systems are on-premise and your VPN gateway forwards traffic without source address translation.

  • DNS resolution: public DNS will not resolve the gateway hostname — you may need to configure an internal DNS server or update a hosts file on the client machine, but your network specialist will know the right approach for your environment
  • Routing: traffic to the gateway's private IP (192.168.250.0/23) needs to be routed through the VPN tunnel — how this is configured depends on your routing setup
  • Allowed IPs: the source IP arriving at the SAP firewall is typically the IP of the client machine — enter that IP in Cloud Portal (10.10.5.20 for a single machine, or a subnet like 10.10.5.0/29 for a group of systems)

Scenario C: On-premise network, VPN Site-to-Site, with NAT on client VPN gateway

Your VPN gateway performs address translation on both source and destination.

  • DNS resolution: the hostname can resolve to any IP that your network routes through the VPN tunnel — it does not need to be the gateway's actual private IP, as your VPN gateway handles the destination translation
  • Routing: traffic to the resolved IP needs to be directed through the VPN tunnel — your network specialist will know how to configure this
  • VPN gateway NAT configuration: your VPN gateway needs to translate the destination IP to the gateway's actual private IP (shown in Cloud Portal alongside the connection string) — without this, traffic will enter the tunnel but not reach the correct endpoint on the SAP side
  • Allowed IPs: the source IP arriving at the SAP firewall is typically the IP of your VPN gateway after source NAT — enter that IP in Cloud Portal

A note on the gateway subnet address space

All private SQL View Gateways in a project share the same private address pool (192.168.250.0/23). If this range conflicts with your existing network addressing, contact SAP Support — changing it requires a service request handled by the SAP support team. Self-service configuration of this address range through Cloud Portal is planned for a future release.

Public access

In public access mode, the gateway is reachable from the internet, protected by a server-level firewall and geographic restrictions. The DNS hostname in the connection URL resolves via public DNS.

Enter the public IP address of the system that will be connecting — as it appears on the internet, after any NAT performed by your network. The same principle applies as with VPN: it is the source IP as seen by the gateway firewall that must match, not necessarily the machine's internal IP.

Use public access only when VPN is not available. Keep the allowlist as narrow as possible and review it regularly.

TLS 1.2+

All connections — VPN or public — are required to use TLS 1.2 or higher. Standard SQL drivers and clients honour this by default; if you are using a custom or non-standard driver, verify that TLS 1.2+ is configured explicitly.

Troubleshooting connectivity

If a connection fails despite correct credentials and a RUNNING gateway:

  1. DNS: verify the gateway hostname resolves correctly. In VPN mode, public DNS will not resolve the hostname — check your internal DNS or hosts file.
  2. Routing: verify traffic to the resolved IP is routed through the VPN tunnel and not dropped or sent to the public internet.
  3. Allowed IPs: verify the source IP as seen by the SAP firewall matches what you entered in Cloud Portal. If unsure, ask your network team to trace the connection.
  4. NAT configuration (Scenario C): verify your VPN gateway correctly translates the destination IP to the gateway's private IP.
  5. Gateway status: must be RUNNING — not UPDATING, BLOCKED, or ERROR.
  6. Credential status: must be active and not deactivated.
  7. TLS: verify TLS 1.2+ is in use.
  8. Driver connection policy: for VPN mode, use the server-side connection policy. Forcing a non-default connection mode can cause timeouts on private connections — the default driver behavior is correct.

Cost

SQL View Gateway is billed through Consumption Credits — SAP's pay-per-use model. The cost depends on the compute tier of each gateway, measured in Capacity Units per month.

The key inputs to your cost estimate:

  • How many gateways do you need, and in which environments? A production gateway warrants a higher tier than a development one.
  • What vCore tier for each? Start at the minimum allowed (25% of primary vCores) and scale based on workload.
  • Can workloads share a gateway? Multiple credentials on a single gateway cost the same as one — the cost driver is the gateway tier, not the number of credentials.

SAP provides a Capacity Estimator that walks through this more precisely. The practical rule of thumb: start at the minimum tier and move up if CPU utilization consistently approaches 80% under normal load.

If you do not yet have Consumption Credits, this is a good moment to consider making the switch from your current billing model. Consumption Credits give you fine-grained cost visibility and the flexibility to scale usage up or down without renegotiating your contract.

A few operational realities

Gateway availability — SQL View Gateway is not classified as a business-critical service. During maintenance windows, system initialization, or database backup operations, the gateway enters a temporarily blocked state. Consumers should be designed to handle brief unavailability gracefully — retry logic, not an assumption that the connection is always-on.

All access is logged — every connection event and every credential management action is recorded in an audit trail. This is available in Cloud Portal and useful for both compliance and troubleshooting unexpected access patterns.

Role changes take effect on next deployment — if you update a role definition and deploy, existing credentials automatically reflect the new data scope. You don't need to recreate credentials. If you remove a role entirely and a credential was assigned to it, that credential loses all data access until it is reassigned to another role.

Credential deactivation vs deletion — SQL View Gateway supports two ways to revoke access: deactivation, which suspends access without removing the credential configuration, and deletion, which is permanent. Deactivation is useful for temporary access suspension, maintenance windows, or rotating service accounts; deletion is appropriate when the credential is no longer needed at all. Note: credential deactivation and reactivation are not yet available in Cloud Portal — this capability is coming in the near future. For now, if you need to suspend access to a credential, delete it and recreate it when access should be restored.

What's next

The next post in this series covers integration: how to connect SAP Commerce Cloud to SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud using SQL View Gateway — end to end.

Learn more

  • Business overview video
  • Configuration guide video
  • Roles and data scopes video
  • Resource planning and estimating guide
  • Capacity Estimator documentation
  • Data extraction documentation
  • SQL View Gateway documentation

entry.title: Connecting SAP Commerce Cloud to SAP Datasphere Using SQL View Gateway
entry.content:
Connecting SAP Commerce Cloud to SAP Datasphere Using SQL View Gateway

This is the third and final post in a series on SQL View Gateway. The first post introduced SQL View Gateway and explained why it exists. The second post covered end-to-end configuration. This post shows how to connect SQL View Gateway to SAP Datasphere — and what to do with the data once it arrives there.

SAP Datasphere can consume Commerce data from SQL View Gateway in several ways. This post walks through the full integration — from prerequisites to live data in Datasphere — and covers three ingestion approaches in detail.

One important note before we start: the delta replication approach described in this post is a proof of concept, not an official SAP recommendation. It is one way to solve the problem of incremental data synchronization, and it works — but there are other valid approaches, and your implementation may look different depending on your requirements and Datasphere expertize.

Prerequisites

This post assumes SQL View Gateway is already up and running. Specifically, you will need:

  • A provisioned, RUNNING SQL View Gateway in Cloud Portal (see the previous post)
  • A deployed role with the Commerce types you want to bring into Datasphere
  • A credential assigned to that role — this is what the Data Provisioning Agent will use to authenticate
  • An SAP Datasphere tenant

Architecture overview

The integration follows a straightforward path:

  SAP Commerce Cloud
  (SQL View Gateway)
          │ SQL / TLS 1.2+
          ▼
  SAP BTP Data Provisioning Agent (dpAgent)
          │
          ▼
  SAP Datasphere
          │
          ▼
  SAP Analytics Cloud

The Data Provisioning Agent (dpAgent) is the bridge. It is a lightweight application that you deploy and run yourself — either in a cloud environment or on a server in your internal network. It connects to SQL View Gateway using standard SQL credentials and makes the data available to SAP Datasphere as a remote source.

Where you run the dpAgent determines your connectivity model:

  • Cloud-hosted dpAgent (e.g. a VM in Azure) — connects to SQL View Gateway over the public internet; use public access mode on the gateway
  • On-premise or VPN-connected dpAgent — connects through your VPN tunnel; use VPN mode on the gateway

Either approach works. The choice depends on your network architecture and security requirements.

Step 1: Set up the Data Provisioning Agent

Setting up the dpAgent is covered in the SAP Datasphere documentation. At a high level:

  1. Create a Data Provisioning Agent entry in SAP Datasphere: go to System → Configuration → Data Integration and add a new agent. Note the agent token — you will need it during agent setup.
  2. Download and install the dpAgent on your chosen host.
  3. Start the agent and register it with your Datasphere tenant using the token from step 1.
  4. Verify the agent appears as connected in Datasphere.

Once the agent is running, two network requirements must be met:

  • The dpAgent host must be able to reach SQL View Gateway — ensure there are no firewall rules on the dpAgent host or its network that block outbound SQL traffic to the gateway endpoint.
  • SQL View Gateway must allow the dpAgent's IP — in Cloud Portal, update the gateway's allowed IPs to include the IP address from which the dpAgent will connect (its public IP if connecting over the internet, or its internal IP if connecting through VPN). This was covered in detail in the previous post.

Step 2: Create a connection in SAP Datasphere

With the dpAgent running, create a connection that points to your SQL View Gateway:

  1. In SAP Datasphere, go to Connections and create a new connection.
  2. Select Microsoft SQL Server as the connection type (SQL View Gateway exposes a standard SQL Server endpoint).
  3. Enter the connection details from Cloud Portal:
    • Host: the server name from the connection string
    • Port: as shown in Cloud Portal (typically 1433)
    • Database: the database name from the connection string
    • Username and Password: the credential you created in Cloud Portal
  4. Select the dpAgent you registered in Step 1.
  5. Test the connection and save.

If the connection test fails, work through the connectivity troubleshooting checklist from the previous post — the most common causes are allowed IP misconfiguration and DNS resolution issues.

Step 3: Choose your ingestion approach

Once the connection is established, you have three options for how to consume Commerce data in Datasphere. The right choice depends on your use case.

Option 1: Remote Tables (real-time)

Remote Tables give you a live view of Commerce data — queries run directly against SQL View Gateway on demand, with no data copied into Datasphere. This is the simplest option to set up.

In SAP Datasphere, go to Data Builder → Import Remote Tables, select your SQL View Gateway connection, and choose the views you want to expose. Datasphere creates a remote table for each view. You can use these tables directly in views, models, and stories.

Best for: dashboards that need up-to-date data, low-volume queries, prototyping.

Trade-off: every query hits SQL View Gateway. Under heavy load or complex queries, this puts pressure on the gateway — consider gateway sizing carefully, and keep in mind that a dedicated gateway per use case (as described in the previous post) isolates this load from other consumers.

Option 2: Full replication (Data Flows)

Data Flows copy Commerce data into local tables in Datasphere on a scheduled basis. After the initial load, queries run against Datasphere's own storage — no ongoing load on the gateway.

In Data Builder, create a Data Flow that reads from a remote table and writes to a local table. Schedule it to run at whatever frequency your use case requires.

Best for: bulk analytical workloads, historical trend analysis, scenarios where query performance matters more than data freshness.

Trade-off: data is only as fresh as the last scheduled run. For large tables, full reloads can be expensive — which is where delta replication helps.

Option 3: Delta replication

Delta replication copies only the records that have changed since the last run. This is significantly more efficient than full replication for large, frequently updated tables — but it requires more setup.

The approach described here uses creationtime and modifiedtime columns — which are present on every SAP Commerce type — to detect new and updated records. It is one possible implementation, not the only way to approach this problem.

One important limitation: this mechanism does not detect deleted records. A record replicated to Datasphere will remain there permanently, even if it is subsequently deleted in Commerce. Depending on your use case, this may or may not be acceptable. Handling deletions requires additional implementation on the Commerce side — for example, soft-deleting records by marking them with a flag rather than removing them from the database, and propagating that flag through the replication. The right approach depends on your requirements and is outside the scope of this post.

Setting up delta replication

How the mechanism works

The delta replication is driven by two independent clocks that work together.

The DeltaImports table acts as a synchronization manifest. Each row represents one data source to replicate. The run column holds a counter — it tracks how many times this source should have been synchronized. You control how often a source is synchronized by controlling how often this counter is incremented. A Data Flow in Datasphere handles the incrementing on a schedule you define per source.

The HANA scheduler job runs independently at a fixed interval (for example, every 10 minutes). On each run, it scans the DeltaImports table and compares each row's run value against the last recorded run in delta_import_control. If run is higher than last_run, it means at least one synchronisation is overdue — so it replicates all records created or modified since the last run, then updates last_run to match.

This two-clock design means synchronization frequency is controlled by how often you increment run, but the actual replication happens at the HANA scheduler's cadence. In practice, data will be at most one scheduler interval late relative to the run counter. For example: if a Data Flow increments run every 15 minutes, and the HANA job runs every 10 minutes, data will be synchronized approximately every 15 minutes — but never more than 10 minutes late relative to when the increment happened.

This also means you can replicate different sources at different frequencies simply by scheduling their Data Flows differently — without changing the HANA job at all.

One practical implication for sizing your scheduler interval: data will never be fresher than the HANA job schedule, regardless of how frequently you increment run. If the HANA job runs every 10 minutes, data will be at most 10 minutes stale — incrementing run every minute will not change that. Set the HANA job schedule to the maximum acceptable data latency for your use case, and treat it as the ceiling on data freshness for the entire delta replication setup.

At the same time, avoid setting the interval too aggressively. Each run of the HANA job executes queries and upsert operations against your Datasphere schema — running it every few seconds would introduce unnecessary load on the HANA side. Delta replication is designed for scheduled synchronization scenarios where data latency of minutes is acceptable, not seconds. If you need truly real-time access to Commerce data, Remote Tables are the better fit.

Overview

The mechanism works as follows:

  1. A control table in Datasphere tracks the last successful synchronization timestamp per data source.
  2. A scheduled job runs at regular intervals and upserts records that have been created or modified since the last run.
  3. A history table records each run for auditing and troubleshooting.

3.1 Create the supporting tables in SAP Datasphere

In SAP Datasphere, go to Configuration → Database Access → Database Analysis Users and create a user with Space Schema Access enabled. Open Database Explorer and connect with this user.

Creating a Database Analysis User automatically creates a corresponding Open SQL Schema — a dedicated schema in the underlying HANA database where you can create tables, views, and procedures. The schema name is derived from the username and will look something like USERNAME#SCHEMANAME. In the code snippets throughout this section, we use CCV2_DSP#COMMERCE as a placeholder — replace it with the actual schema name shown in your Database Explorer after creating the user.

In the Open SQL Schema, create the following tables:

Control table — tracks the last successful run per data source:

create column table delta_import_control (
      pk BIGINT primary key,
      last_datetime TIMESTAMP not null,
      last_run BIGINT
  );

History table — append-only log of every run:

create column table delta_import_history (
      pk BIGINT primary key generated always as identity,
      delta_import BIGINT not null,
      run_datetime TIMESTAMP not null,
      run_from TIMESTAMP,
      run_to TIMESTAMP not null,
      affected BIGINT not null,
      foreign key (delta_import) references delta_import_control
  );

3.2 Create the DeltaImports entity in Datasphere

The synchronization mechanism is driven by a table called DeltaImports that lives in your Datasphere space. It tells the system which source views to sync and where to put the results.

Create a local table called DeltaImports with the following structure (use the CSN below in Data Builder):

{
    "definitions": {
      "DeltaImports": {
        "kind": "entity",
        "elements": {
          "PK": { "type": "cds.Integer64", "key": true, "notNull": true },
          "src": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 128, "notNull": true },
          "res": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 128, "notNull": true },
          "run": { "type": "cds.Integer64", "notNull": true, "default": { "val": "0" } }
        }
      }
    }
  }
  • src — fully qualified name of the source view in Datasphere (e.g. "MYSPACE"."V_Orders")
  • res — fully qualified name of the target local table (e.g. "CCV2_DSP#COMMERCE"."Orders") — replace with your Open SQL Schema name
  • run — a counter incremented on each run; used to trigger the Data Flow

Then create a 1:1 view on DeltaImports called V_DeltaImports and mark it Exposed for analytics. This view is what the scheduled Data Flow reads.

3.3 Create the HANA procedures

Back in Database Explorer, create the following stored procedures in your Open SQL Schema.

prepare_incremental_import — called once per data source to create the target local table and grant the necessary permissions to your Datasphere space:

create or replace procedure prepare_incremental_import(
      in src NVARCHAR(128),
      in res NVARCHAR(128)
  ) as
  begin
      declare sch nvarchar(128);
      select CURRENT_SCHEMA into sch from dummy;

      exec 'create row table "' || :sch || '"."' || :res || '" as (select * from ' || :src || ')';
      exec 'alter table "' || :sch || '"."' || :res || '" add primary key ("PK")';

      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'SELECT',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'UPDATE',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'DELETE',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'INSERT',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
  end;

Replace YOUR_SPACE_ID with your actual Datasphere space ID.

incremental_import — the core synchronization procedure. For a given source/target pair, it upserts all records modified or created since the last run:

create or replace procedure incremental_import(
      in pk bigint,
      in src NVARCHAR(128),
      in res NVARCHAR(128),
      in run bigint
  ) as
  begin
      declare dt, curr_dt timestamp;
      declare query nvarchar(2000);
      declare affected bigint;

      select UTCTOLOCAL(CURRENT_UTCTIMESTAMP, 'Etc/UTC') into curr_dt from dummy;
      select ifnull((select last_datetime from delta_import_control where pk = :pk), null)
          into dt from dummy;

      query = 'upsert ' || :res || ' SELECT * from ' || :src
          || ' where "modifiedtime" < ''' || :curr_dt || ''''
          || ' and "creationtime" < ''' || :curr_dt || '''';

      IF dt is not null THEN
          query = query || ' and ("modifiedtime" > ''' || :dt
              || ''' or "creationtime" > ''' || :dt || ''')';
      END IF;

      exec query;
      SELECT ::ROWCOUNT INTO affected from dummy;

      upsert delta_import_control(pk, last_datetime, last_run)
          values (:pk, :curr_dt, :run) WITH PRIMARY KEY;
      insert into delta_import_history(delta_import, run_datetime, run_from, run_to, affected)
          values (:pk, :curr_dt, :dt, :curr_dt, :affected);
  end;

incremental_imports — the orchestrator. Reads V_DeltaImports and calls incremental_import for each entry that has a new run value:

create or replace procedure incremental_imports as
  begin
      DECLARE pk, run BIGINT;
      DECLARE src, res NVARCHAR(128);
      DECLARE deltas table (
          pk bigint, src nvarchar(128), res nvarchar(128),
          run bigint, last_run bigint
      );
      DECLARE CURSOR c_deltas for select pk, src, res, run, last_run from :deltas;

      deltas =
          select di.pk, di."src", di."res", di."run", dic.last_run
          from "YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_DeltaImports" di
          left outer join delta_import_control dic on di.pk = dic.pk;

      FOR delta AS c_deltas DO
          if :delta.last_run is null or :delta.run > :delta.last_run then
              call incremental_import(:delta.pk, :delta.src, :delta.res, :delta.run);
          end if;
      END FOR;
  end;

Replace YOUR_SPACE_ID with your actual space ID.

3.4 Schedule the synchronization

Create a scheduler job in HANA to run incremental_imports at your desired interval. The example below runs every 10 minutes:

CREATE SCHEDULER JOB do_incremental_imports
      CRON '* * * * * * 00,10,20,30,40,50'
      ENABLE PROCEDURE incremental_imports;

Adjust the schedule to match your freshness requirements and gateway capacity.

3.5 Register a data source for delta replication

For each Commerce type you want to replicate using this mechanism:

  1. In Data Builder, use Import Remote Tables to create a remote table for the SQL View Gateway view (e.g. salesreporting_order).
  2. Create a 1:1 view on that remote table (e.g. V_Orders) and mark it Exposed for analytics.
  3. In Database Explorer, call prepare_incremental_import to create the target table and grant permissions:
CALL prepare_incremental_import(
      '"YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_Orders"',
      'Orders'
  );
  • Insert a row into DeltaImports to register this source:
INSERT INTO "YOUR_SPACE_ID"."DeltaImports"(PK, src, res, run)
  VALUES (
      1,
      '"YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_Orders"',
      '"CCV2_DSP#COMMERCE"."Orders"', -- replace with your Open SQL Schema name
      0
  );

  • Create a Data Flow to trigger the synchronization.

    This Data Flow serves one specific purpose: it increments the run counter in DeltaImports for a given source. That increment is the signal that tells incremental_imports to process this source on the next scheduler run.

    The Data Flow has three nodes:

    • Source: reads from the DeltaImports local table
    • Projection: filters to the row where src matches your source view, and adds a calculated column run_inc = run + 1
    • Target: writes back to DeltaImports using upsert mode (update if exists, insert if not)

    To create it in Data Builder:

    1. Create a new Data Flow.
    2. Add DeltaImports as a source node.
    3. Add a Projection node after the source. In the projection:
      • Add a filter: "src" = '"YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_Orders"'
      • Map all columns through unchanged (PK, src, res)
      • Add a calculated column run_inc with expression "run" + 1
    4. Add DeltaImports as a target node. Set the write mode to Upsert. Map run_inc → run, and all other columns through unchanged.
    5. Save and activate the Data Flow.

    The JSON below shows a complete example for OrderEntries — you can import it directly into Data Builder and adapt it for your source:

{
    "dataflows": {
      "DF_OrderEntries": {
        "kind": "sap.dis.dataflow",
        "contents": {
          "processes": {
            "source1": {
              "component": "com.sap.database.table.consumer",
              "metadata": {
                "label": "DeltaImports",
                "config": {
                  "service": "HANA",
                  "hanaConnection": { "configurationType": "Configuration Manager", "connectionID": "$DWC" },
                  "qualifiedName": "DeltaImports",
                  "dwcEntity": "DeltaImports"
                }
              }
            },
            "projection1": {
              "component": "com.sap.dataflow.projection",
              "metadata": {
                "label": "Projection 1",
                "config": {
                  "expression": "\"src\" = '\"YOUR_SPACE_ID\".\"V_Orders\"'",
                  "attributeMappings": [
                    { "target": "PK", "expression": "\"PK\"" },
                    { "target": "src", "expression": "\"src\"" },
                    { "target": "res", "expression": "\"res\"" },
                    { "target": "run_inc", "expression": "\"run\" + 1" }
                  ]
                }
              }
            },
            "target1": {
              "component": "com.sap.database.table.producer",
              "metadata": {
                "label": "DeltaImports",
                "config": {
                  "service": "HANA",
                  "hanaConnection": { "configurationType": "Configuration Manager", "connectionID": "$DWC" },
                  "qualifiedName": "DeltaImports",
                  "dwcEntity": "DeltaImports",
                  "mode": "append",
                  "upsert": true,
                  "attributeMappings": [
                    { "expression": "\"PK\"", "target": "PK" },
                    { "expression": "\"src\"", "target": "src" },
                    { "expression": "\"res\"", "target": "res" },
                    { "expression": "\"run_inc\"", "target": "run" }
                  ]
                }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }

Replace YOUR_SPACE_ID and V_Orders with your actual space ID and source view name. Create a separate Data Flow for each Commerce type you are replicating.

Repeat steps 1–5 for each Commerce type you want to replicate.

Step 4: Model your data in Datasphere

Once your Commerce data is flowing into Datasphere — whether via Remote Tables, Data Flows, or delta replication — you can start building your data model.

The typical pattern for Commerce analytics:

  • Fact tables: transactional data — Orders, OrderEntries, ReturnRequests
  • Dimension tables: reference data — Products, Categories, Customers
  • Analytical models: join facts and dimensions into consumption views

This is standard Datasphere modeling and is covered in the SAP Datasphere documentation.

Step 5: Connect to SAP Analytics Cloud

With your data modeled in Datasphere, connecting SAP Analytics Cloud is straightforward — SAC has native connectivity to Datasphere. From there you can build stories, dashboards, and planning models on top of your Commerce data.

SAC–Datasphere integration is covered in the SAP Analytics Cloud documentation.

A few things to keep in mind

This is a proof of concept. The delta replication approach shown here is one way to solve incremental synchronization. It worked well in testing, but it is not an official SAP pattern. Your implementation may differ — for example, you might use Datasphere's built-in replication flows, a different change detection mechanism, or a third-party ETL tool. Treat the code snippets as a starting point, not a blueprint.

Data privacy and data hygiene are your responsibility. SQL View Gateway exposes raw Commerce data — it restricts access to certain internal technical types, but it does not sanitize or validate the content of the data it exposes. If your Commerce data includes values entered by end users (for example, free-text fields, addresses, or customer-supplied identifiers), treat those values as untrusted input. Validate and sanitize them before using them in downstream queries, models, or applications — SQL injection and other injection attacks are possible if user-supplied values are passed through without inspection. Separately, ensure that what you expose complies with your data processing obligations, consent requirements, and any cross-border data transfer rules applicable to your deployment.

Credential management. The dpAgent credential is a long-lived technical account. Rotate its password regularly, restrict its role to only the types needed for Datasphere, and treat the credential with the same care as any other service account.

Gateway sizing. If you use Remote Tables, every Datasphere query hits the gateway directly. Monitor gateway CPU utilization and consider a dedicated gateway for Datasphere traffic to isolate it from other consumers.

Learn more

  • SAP Datasphere documentation
  • Data Provisioning Agent documentation
  • SAP Analytics Cloud documentation
  • Business overview video
  • Configuration guide video
  • SQL View Gateway documentation

entry.title: Ensuring Quality in BADI Implementations with ABAP Unit
entry.content:
Introduction

In a previous article, I described how to identify and implement the BADI FIN_PROFIT_CENTER_VALIDATION to enforce business rules during Profit Center maintenance.

However, implementing a solution is only part of the job. Once the enhancement is in production, new requirements, bug fixes, and refactoring activities can easily introduce regressions if the validation logic is not properly tested.

This is where ABAP Unit becomes essential. By creating automated tests for enhancement implementations, we can verify business rules consistently and gain confidence when evolving the solution over time.

In this article, I will share a practical example of how I tested a BADI implementation using ABAP Unit based on a real-world validation scenario.

Why Testing a BAdI Matters

BADI implementations often contain critical business logic. In many projects, this logic evolves gradually as new requirements are added. Without automated tests, every change introduces a risk of unexpected side effects.

The validation implemented in FIN_PROFIT_CENTER_VALIDATION ensures that the Segment value remains consistent with the last digit of the Profit Center. Whenever this rule is violated, an error message must be generated and the maintenance process must be blocked.

The business rule is relatively simple:

Last DigitExpected Segment
0GERAL
1ATO
2NAO_ATO

Although the rule itself is straightforward, it is a perfect candidate for automated testing because it contains clearly defined inputs and expected outcomes.

Test Class Design

Before creating individual test cases, I structured the test class to maximize readability and reduce duplication. The objective was to make each test focus only on the business rule being verified.

The class uses standard setup and teardown methods to create and release the class under test. This guarantees that each test executes in isolation and avoids side effects from previous executions.

METHOD setup.
  CREATE OBJECT me->f_cut.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD teardown.
  FREE me->f_cut.
  REFRESH me->messages.
ENDMETHOD.

The BADI method expects a FIN_ENH_PROFIT_CENTER structure as input and returns validation messages through the CT_MESSAGES parameter. Since several test cases share the same execution flow, I introduced a helper method named ASSERT_NO_VALIDATION_ERROR.

This helper method prepares test data, executes the BADI, handles unexpected runtime exceptions, and verifies that no validation message was raised.

METHOD assert_no_validation_error.
    " Given
    DATA(profit_center_data) = VALUE fin_enh_profit_center( validityenddate	               = `19000101`
                                                            controllingareaforedit         = `SI01`
                                                            profitcenterforedit	           = profitcenter
                                                            validityenddateforedit         = `99991231`
                                                            validitystartdate	             = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentername               = `Z999999999`
                                                            profitctrresponsiblepersonname = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcentercreationdate       = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentercreatedbyuser	     = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcenterstandardhierarchy	 = `0101`
                                                            segment                        = segment ).

    " When
    TRY.
        me->f_cut->if_badi_fin_pc_validation~execute( EXPORTING is_profit_center = profit_center_data
                                                      CHANGING  ct_messages      = me->messages ).
      CATCH cx_ble_runtime_error INTO DATA(lx_error).
        cl_abap_unit_assert=>fail( msg = lx_error->get_text( ) ).
    ENDTRY.

    " Then
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_initial( act = me->messages ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Using a helper method significantly reduced code duplication and allowed the individual test methods to remain concise and focused on their specific scenario.

Implementing the Test Scenario

To validate the implementation comprehensively, I created tests covering three different categories of behavior.

The first category verifies situations where the last digit of the Profit Center is not one of the supported values (0, 1, or 2). In these cases, the validation should simply be ignored.

METHOD ignore_when_last_digit_not_012.
  ...
ENDMETHOD.

The second category focuses on invalid combinations. In the following example, the Profit Center ends with 0, which means the expected Segment should be GERAL. Since the supplied value is ATO, the validation must generate an error message.

METHOD error_when_last_digit_is_0.
    " Given
    DATA(profit_center_data) = VALUE fin_enh_profit_center( validityenddate	               = `19000101`
                                                            controllingareaforedit         = `SI01`
                                                            profitcenterforedit	           = `Z999999990`
                                                            validityenddateforedit         = `99991231`
                                                            validitystartdate	             = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentername               = `Z999999999`
                                                            profitctrresponsiblepersonname = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcentercreationdate       = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentercreatedbyuser	     = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcenterstandardhierarchy	 = `0101`
                                                            segment                        = `ATO` ).

    " When
    TRY.
        me->f_cut->if_badi_fin_pc_validation~execute( EXPORTING is_profit_center = profit_center_data
                                                      CHANGING  ct_messages      = me->messages ).
      CATCH cx_ble_runtime_error INTO DATA(lx_error).
        cl_abap_unit_assert=>fail( msg = lx_error->get_text( ) ).
    ENDTRY.

    " Then
    DATA(expected_messages) = VALUE fin_enh_pc_message_t( ( msgid = `ZFIN`
                                                            msgty = `E`
                                                            msgno = 001
                                                            msgv1 = `GERAL` ) ).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( act = me->messages          " Data object with current value
                                        exp = expected_messages ).  " Data object with expected type
  ENDMETHOD.

Finally, positive test cases verify that valid combinations do not generate any validation messages.

METHOD validate_when_last_digit_is_1.

  me->assert_no_validation_error(
    profitcenter = 'Z999999991'
    segment      = 'ATO' ).

ENDMETHOD.
METHOD validate_when_last_digit_is_2.

  me->assert_no_validation_error(
    profitcenter = 'Z999999992'
    segment      = 'NAO_ATO' ).

ENDMETHOD.

Lessons Learned and Clean ABAP Considerations

One interesting aspect of this exercise is how testability influenced the overall design of the solution. The need to create clear and maintainable tests encouraged the use of small methods, descriptive names, and reusable helper methods.

The resulting test class follows several Clean ABAP principles:

  • Small and focused test methods
  • Meaningful and descriptive names
  • Helper methods to eliminate duplication
  • Explicit use of me-> for clarity
  • Minimal shared state between tests

Another takeaway is that even relatively small BADI implementations benefit from automated testing. The effort required to create the tests was modest, while the long-term benefits in maintainability and confidence are substantial.

Final Thoughts

Implementing a BAdI is only the first step. Ensuring that the implementation remains reliable over time is equally important.

ABAP Unit provided a simple and effective way to validate the business rules implemented in FIN_PROFIT_CENTER_VALIDATION while making future changes much safer.

If you are developing custom validations through enhancement spots or BAdIs, I strongly recommend investing a little extra time in automated tests—it pays off quickly.

👉I would be interested to hear how other teams are testing their BAdI implementations and validation frameworks.

entry.title: Re: How to reverse/cancel the reservation document in the...
entry.content:
In the subcontracting process, once you perform the ASSIGN step, SAP creates a reservation document to link components to the subcontracting purchase order. Reversing or “unassigning” that reservation is not straightforward because SAP treats the assignment as a consumption requirement.

Use transaction MB22 to delete or adjust the reservation line items. If the reservation has already been partially consumed, you may only be able to adjust the remaining quantity. If goods issue has already been posted against the reservation, reverse it with MBST or MIGO → Cancellation. Then adjust/delete the reservation in MB22.

entry.title: Re: Sale order number is not displaying in CM25
entry.content: The blank field in CM25 is not a layout issue but a data issue. CM25 displays capacity requirements from planned orders, production orders, or reservations. The sales order number is only populated if the requirement is directly linked to a sales order (make-to-order scenario). If the requirement comes from a planned order without sales order reference, or from a stock order (make-to-stock), the sales order field will remain blank. Adding the field in CY38 only makes the column visible; it does not guarantee that the field will be filled.
